# Systems Biology and Synthetic Biology in Advanced Agriculture and Food Systems

50

Total downloads

2,135

Total views and downloads

Submit your idea

Select the journal/section where you want your idea to be submitted:

## About this Research Topic

This Research Topic is still accepting articles.

### Background

Meeting the challenge of feeding a growing global population sustainably amidst increasing extreme weather events requires innovative solutions. Systems Biology and Synthetic Biology offer a promising avenue to revolutionize agriculture. By engineering biological systems, we can enhance crop resilience, nutrient uptake, and yield under adverse conditions. Additionally, Systems Biology and Synthetic Biology enable precise manufacturing of valuable molecules and high-value compounds, while also facilitating micro-scale production processes that minimize waste. Responsible biotechnological interventions can further reduce food waste and synthesize essential nutrients without harming the environment. Integrating both Systems Biology and Synthetic biology into agriculture holds the potential to create a sustainable food system that meets the world's nutritional needs while preserving our planet for future generations.   
  
Our objective is to spotlight the multifaceted applications of Systems Biology and Synthetic Biology as potent tools for advanced agriculture and food systems. This includes, but is not limited to:  
  
**Food & Nutrition:** from detecting food spoilage and addressing nutritional deficiencies to fostering sustainable aquaculture and developing non-animal protein sources, we’re looking for research that spans a wide array of critical challenges.   
  
**Biomanufacturing in Agriculture:**  we also explore innovations in food packaging and storage, micro-scale manufacturing, fabric production, and tissue engineering, including the regeneration of new skin and organs. Our focus extends to fragrance production, clean dyes, pigments, and sustainable mining practices.   
  
**Agriculture:**  we delve into the development of biological fertilizers, microbial nitrogen fixation, and strategies for enhancing crop yields, stress tolerance, disease and pest resistance, while preserving soil health. We aim to showcase the transformative potential of Systems Biology and Synthetic Biology across diverse sectors, driving sustainable solutions for a better future.  
  
**Exemplary (iGEM) Projects:**   
**2023 iGEM Kyoto (Japan)**  was nominated for the Best Agriculture Project award for proposing a new solution that prevents crop damage by utilizing an engineered E. coli for sustained production and release of repellent molecules.   
  
**2023 iGEM Tec-Chihuahua (Mexico)**  was nominated for the Best Food & Nutrition Project award for pioneering non-antibiotic treatment for bovine mastitis, an inflammatory udder infection caused by gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, impacting milk production, causing wastage and rising prices.  
  
**2023 iGEM Leiden (The Netherlands)**  was nominated for the Best Biomanufacturing Project award for working on Methylorubrum extorquens, a non-model organism that can grow on green methanol to sustainably produce biodegradable plastic, a solution that promises to transform agriculture.  
  
Learn more about  [iGEM Projects](https://projects.igem.org/) .  
  
**Scope:**   
This Research Topic encourages submissions from the iGEM community and researchers around the globe who work in areas intersecting Systems Biology, Synthetic Biology and advanced agriculture and food systems. Investigators who pioneer the development of potent tools integrating these fields with a focus on sustainable food systems are particularly encouraged to submit their research. We invite researchers to share their findings in the form of original research, reviews, perspectives, technology and code, or novel methods and/or their novel applications.  
  
Please note manuscripts that are purely synthetic without a systems approach (i.e. at multiple levels/scales/time points or using multiple methods such as multi-omics) will be considered in our newly launched Frontiers in Synthetic Biology. Please see our  [2023 iGEM collection](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/65859/igem-2023-collection)  for an idea of systems level papers.

![Research Topic Research topic image](https://www.frontiersin.org/image/researchtopic/65849)

## Frequently asked questions

Frontiers' Research Topics are collaborative hubs built around an emerging theme.Defined, managed, and led by renowned researchers, they bring communities together around a shared area of interest to stimulate collaboration and innovation.

Unlike section journals, which serve established specialty communities, Research Topics are pioneer hubs, responding to the evolving scientific landscape and catering to new communities.

The goal of Frontiers' publishing program is to empower research communities to actively steer the course of scientific publishing. Our program was implemented as a three-part unit with fixed field journals, flexible specialty sections, and dynamically emerging Research Topics, connecting communities of different sizes and maturity.

Research Topics originate from the scientific community. Many of our Research Topics are suggested by existing editorial board members who have identified critical challenges or areas of interest in their field.

As an editor, Research Topics will help you build your journal, as well as your community, around emerging, cutting-edge research. As research trailblazers, Research Topics attract high-quality submissions from leading experts all over the world.

A thriving Research Topic can potentially evolve into a new specialty section if there is sustained interest and a growing community around it.

Each Research Topic must be approved by the specialty chief editor, and it falls under the editorial oversight of our editorial boards, supported by our in-house research integrity team. The same standards and rigorous peer review processes apply to articles published as part of a Research Topic as for any other article we publish.

In 2023, 80% of the Research Topics we published were edited or co-edited by our editorial board members, who are already familiar with their journal's scope, ethos, and publishing model. All other topics are guest edited by leaders in their field, each vetted and formally approved by the specialty chief editor.

Publishing your article within a Research Topic with other related articles increases its discoverability and visibility, which can lead to more views, downloads, and citations. Research Topics grow dynamically as more published articles are added, causing frequent revisiting, and further visibility.

As Research Topics are multidisciplinary, they are cross-listed in several fields and section journals – increasing your reach even more and giving you the chance to expand your network and collaborate with researchers in different fields, all focusing on expanding knowledge around the same important topic.

Our larger Research Topics are also converted into ebooks and receive social media promotion from our digital marketing team.

Frontiers offers multiple article types, but it will depend on the field and section journals in which the Research Topic will be featured. The available article types for a Research Topic will appear in the drop-down menu during the submission process.

[Check available article types here](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/systems-biology/for-authors/article-types)

Yes, we would love to hear your ideas for a topic. Most of our Research Topics are community-led and suggested by researchers in the field. Our in-house editorial team will contact you to talk about your idea and whether you’d like to edit the topic. If you’re an early-stage researcher, we will offer you the opportunity to coordinate your topic, with the support of a senior researcher as the topic editor.

[Suggest your topic here](https://researchtopics.frontiersin.org/submit)

A team of guest editors (called topic editors) lead their Research Topic. This editorial team oversees the entire process, from the initial topic proposal to calls for participation, the peer review, and final publications.

The team may also include topic coordinators, who help the topic editors send calls for participation, liaise with topic editors on abstracts, and support contributing authors. In some cases, they can also be assigned as reviewers.

As a topic editor (TE), you will take the lead on all editorial decisions for the Research Topic, starting with defining its scope. This allows you to curate research around a topic that interests you, bring together different perspectives from leading researchers across different fields and shape the future of your field.

You will choose your team of co-editors, curate a list of potential authors, send calls for participation and oversee the peer review process, accepting or recommending rejection for each manuscript submitted.

As a topic editor, you're supported at every stage by our in-house team. You will be assigned a single point of contact to help you on both editorial and technical matters. Your topic is managed through our user-friendly online platform, and the peer review process is supported by our industry-first AI review assistant (AIRA).

If you’re an early-stage researcher, we will offer you the opportunity to coordinate your topic, with the support of a senior researcher as the topic editor. This provides you with valuable editorial experience, improving your ability to critically evaluate research articles and enhancing your understanding of the quality standards and requirements for scientific publishing, as well as the opportunity to discover new research in your field, and expand your professional network.

Yes, certificates can be issued on request. We are happy to provide a certificate for your contribution to editing a successful Research Topic.

Research Topics thrive on collaboration and their multi-disciplinary approach around emerging, cutting-edge themes, attract leading researchers from all over the world.

As a topic editor, you can set the timeline for your Research Topic, and we will work with you at your pace. Typically, Research Topics are online and open for submissions within a few weeks and remain open for participation for 6 – 12 months. Individual articles within a Research Topic are published as soon as they are ready.

Find out more [about our Research Topics](https://www.frontiersin.org/about/research-topics)

Our fee support program ensures that all articles that pass peer review, including those published in Research Topics, can benefit from open access – regardless of the author's field or funding situation.

Authors and institutions with insufficient funding can apply for a discount on their publishing fees. A [fee support application form](https://frontiersin.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9YqUCmY73k4Gm0u) is available on our website.

In line with our mission to promote healthy lives on a healthy planet, we do not provide printed materials. All our articles and ebooks are available under a CC-BY license, so you can share and print copies.

--- SOURCE 3: Synthetic biology: A powerful booster for future agriculture ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065

SUMMARY:
This perspective summarizes the application status and future development of synthetic biology in agriculture from the aspects of plant breeding, photosynthetic system, nitrogen fixation, and microorganisms. The application of synthetic biology in agriculture shows the potential of transforming metabolic pathways, genetic circuits and plant architectures in crop improvement.5 Meanwhile, the application of synthetic biology in microorganisms plays a role in sustainable agriculture, such as biofertilization, biostimulation and biocontrol.6 In this perspective, we will introduce the development trend of synthetic biology in agriculture including crop breeding, improvement of plant photosynthesis and nitrogen fixation, as well as some applications of microorganisms (Fig.1). Yoshikuni Microbiome engineering for sustainable agriculture: using synthetic biology to enhance nitrogen metabolism in plant-associated microbes  Curr Opin Microbiol, 68 (2022), Article 102172 View PDFView articleView in ScopusGoogle Scholar

Highlights
----------

*   •Synthetic biology has transformed various aspects of agriculture. 
*   •Synthetic biology promote the sustainable development of modern agriculture. 

Abstract
--------

Abstract: Future agricultural development needs to solve the food security crisis caused by the global food shortage and the environmental demand for green and sustainable technology. The vigorous development of [synthetic biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/synthetic-biology "Learn more about synthetic biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has brought new opportunities for modern [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Synthetic biology can transform crops' metabolic pathways and [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") information and involves microorganisms' application in agriculture. Therefore, it has bright prospects in crop breeding and yield increase and ensuring the safety of the agricultural production environment. This perspective summarizes the application status and future development of synthetic biology in agriculture from the aspects of [plant breeding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-breeding "Learn more about plant breeding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [photosynthetic system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/photosystem "Learn more about photosynthetic system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [nitrogen fixation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nitrogen-assimilation "Learn more about nitrogen fixation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and microorganisms.

1. Introduction
---------------

Affected by the explosive growth of the world's population, [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), war and especially the COVID-19 epidemic that has swept the world in the past two years, global food security is currently facing severe threats. In 2022, the [Food and Agriculture Organization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/food-and-agriculture-organization "Learn more about Food and Agriculture Organization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the United Nations (FAO) released the latest research report, The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2022.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib1) According to this report, about one-third of the world's population still [faces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/face "Learn more about faces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") food insecurity. Meanwhile, the yield growth rates for significant crops have plateaued over the past few years, with limited possibilities for increasing [arable land](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/arable-land "Learn more about arable land from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib2) Therefore, there is an urgent need to find a fast and sustainable way to produce more agricultural products and improve crop nutrition on limited arable land to ensure food security in the future.

With the genomics revolution and the rise of system biology, a rigorous engineering discipline called [synthetic biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/synthetic-biology "Learn more about synthetic biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") emerged to create, control, and program cellular behavior. The use of [molecular biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/molecular-biology "Learn more about molecular biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") tools and techniques in synthetic biology to advance the engineering of cellular behavior has become a broad feature of the field, and researchers have already developed a set of common engineering approaches and laboratory practice methods. Synthetic biology has made significant progress in the areas of energy, chemical industry, medicine, food, environment, also including [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). For example, the semi-synthetic route from synthetic biology makes the source of [artemisinin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/artemisinin "Learn more about artemisinin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") more plentiful and stabilizes the supply chain of artemisinin.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib4) Various countries have formulated policies to promote the development of synthetic biology in agriculture ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#tbl1)). The application of synthetic biology in agriculture shows the potential of transforming metabolic pathways, [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") circuits and plant architectures in crop improvement.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib5) Meanwhile, the application of synthetic biology in microorganisms plays a role in [sustainable agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sustainable-agriculture "Learn more about sustainable agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as biofertilization, [biostimulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biostimulation "Learn more about biostimulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and biocontrol.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib6) In this perspective, we will introduce the development trend of synthetic biology in agriculture including crop breeding, improvement of plant [photosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/photosystem "Learn more about photosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [nitrogen fixation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nitrogen-assimilation "Learn more about nitrogen fixation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as well as some applications of microorganisms ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#fig1)).

Table 1. History of synthetic biology in agriculture.

| Countries | Time | Events |
| --- | --- | --- |
| UK | 2012 | A Synthetic Biology Roadmap for the UK (2012) was proposed and the Synthetic Biology Leadership Council (SBLC) was established. |
|  | 2013 | One example of the Innovation and Knowledge Centre (IKC) for synthetic biology, SynbiCITE was established at Imperial College London, The focus includes the application in agriculture such as crop breeding. |
|  | 2016 | UK Synthetic Biology Strategic Plan (2016) was proposed, which focused on translation of the research and commercialization. |
|  | 2020 | SBLC was reorganized to Engineering Biology Leadership Council (EBLC). One of the working groups discussed the market application of agri-food and identified key interventions. |
| USA | 2006 | Synthetic Biology Research Center (SynBERC) was a multi-university research center established to help lay the foundation for synthetic biology. The United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) began to support relevant research in the field of synthetic biology. |
|  | 2011 | Living Foundries program from Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) could use the engineering platform of synthetic biology to produce natural products instead of agricultural production. |
|  | 2016 | Engineering Biology Research Consortium (EBRC) which was founded by members of SynBERC after the funding expires. |
|  | 2019 | Engineering Biology: A Research Roadmap for the Next-Generation Bioeconomy was released by EBRC. Food and agriculture was one of five focused sectors. |
|  | 2020 | EBRC was established Bioindustrial Manufacturing and Design Ecosystem (BioMADE). BioMADE is committed to the bioindustrial manufacturing including agricultural materials. |
| Australia | 2016 | Synthetic Biology Future Science Platform (SynBio FSP) was established by Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation, CSIRO. |
|  | 2021 | CSIRO released Australia's Synthetic Biology Roadmap. In this roadmap, food and agriculture was expected to largest emerging markets for synthetic biology applications. |
| China | 2018 | “Synthetic biology” key program of the National Key R&D Program of China was launched, of which included the projects are related to agriculture. |
|  | 2022 | The 14 th five-year plan for bioeconomy was unveiled. The plan proposes to promote the technological innovation of synthetic biology in agricultural production, and develop biological breeding technologies using synthetic biology. |

Fig.1. Application of synthetic biology in agriculture.

2. Crop domestication and breeding
----------------------------------

The domestication of crops in the past involved fixation of favorable natural variations and took extraordinarily long or even thousands of years based on archeological observations. In contrast, human selection could shorten the time to fix the semi-dwarf alleles in rice and wheat in modern breeding to several decades.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib7) However, the speed still cannot meet the multiple challenges facing today's food supply. Due to the contradiction between population and cultivated land, researchers have made many technical efforts in crop breeding, especially in China. With the development of genomics technology, crop domestication and the speed of crop breeding have been greatly accelerated by manipulating [plant genomes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/plant-genome "Learn more about plant genomes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Based on understanding the plant genome, the work in crop breeding can be divided into three processes: reading, interpreting, and writing.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib7)[Synthetic biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/synthetic-biology "Learn more about Synthetic biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is one of the crucial technologies in writing for plant genome modification.

The technologies of plant genome writing consist of genome editing and genome design. Prof. Jiayang Li constructed a rapid _de novo_ domestication strategy of allotetraploid wild rice and finally generated a high-quality [genome assembly](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/genome-assembly "Learn more about genome assembly from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which could significantly improve food yield and increase adaptability for crops to environmental changes, opening up a new direction for crop breeding.[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib8) One genotype of _Oryza alta_, [polyploid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/polyploid "Learn more about polyploid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") rice 1 (PPR1) was screened and obtained, in this work. The CRISPR/Cas9, base editing, and multiplex editing technologies were applied to improve PPR1 in established transformation and genome editing systems. Rapid _de novo_ domestication of allotetraploid wild rice can be achieved by editing the homologous genes controlling different traits in the genome of PPR1.

As an emerging technology, CRISPR/Cas system has been able to help the development of crop breeding and plant synthetic biology and bring many possibilities for crop improvement. In many recent works, researchers have effectively used CRISPR/Cas tools in plants to form heritable [chromosome rearrangements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromosome-rearrangement "Learn more about chromosome rearrangements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the megabase range in a controlled way, such as inversions and translocations.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib9) For example, the editing using CRISPR/Cas9 of heritable [chromosomal translocations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chromosome-translocation "Learn more about chromosomal translocations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been verified in _A.thaliana_.[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib10) The [reciprocal translocations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/reciprocal-translocation "Learn more about reciprocal translocations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of plant genes can cause semi-sterility, and the targeted induction of translocations can break or stabilize [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") linkages, which can potentially be used in crop breeding.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib9)

Introducing genome design can also improve crop breeding efficiently. Based on extensive studies of potato genome analysis and genome editing in the past, Prof. Sanwen Huang and collaborators used the genome design method to cultivate hybrid potatoes.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib11) The pipeline for this genome design system in diploid potato breeding had four steps, including the starting materials selection for breaking self-incompatibility, the genetic analysis of the S1 population to identify alleles, the development of [inbred lines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/inbred-strain "Learn more about inbred lines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") containing beneficial alleles, and generation of the final vigorous [F1 hybrid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/f1-hybrid "Learn more about F1 hybrid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Genome design is proved to be a new technology for potato breeding and reproduction and disruptive innovation to the potato industry.

3. Photosynthesis and nitrogen fixation
---------------------------------------

### 3.1. Photosynthetic system modification

[Photosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/photosystem "Learn more about Photosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is the source of energy for crops and is also the primary determinant of crop yield. [Calvin cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/carbon-dioxide-fixation "Learn more about Calvin cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a part of photosynthesis, and most plants will fix the absorbed CO 2 to a [pentose](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/pentose "Learn more about pentose from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") molecule ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP) through this cycle by [enzyme](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme "Learn more about enzyme from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reaction. The enzyme that catalyzes the initiation step is RuBP carboxylase (Rubisco). Through screening and modification, transplanting high-efficiency Rubisco enzymes from other sources into existing crops is relatively straightforward. For example, Prins et al. investigated Rubisco from 25 genotypes of the [Triticeae](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/triticeae "Learn more about Triticeae from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") tribe. They found that Rubisco from wild relatives of [bread wheat](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/triticum-aestivum "Learn more about bread wheat from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (_Triticum aestivum_) was calculated to increase carbon uptake rates by 20% upon substituting native Rubisco in agriculturally used wheat.[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib12)

However, regardless of how to improve the [enzyme activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/enzyme-activity "Learn more about enzyme activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of Rubisco, the overall [carbon fixation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/carbon-fixation "Learn more about carbon fixation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") efficiency is still limited by the natural metabolic pathway itself. Therefore, to increase efficiency to a great extent, it may be necessary to design a new [carbon fixation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/carbon-fixation "Learn more about carbon fixation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pathway, but this is undoubtedly more challenging. Prof. Tobias Erb reported the first synthetic pathway, the crotonyl–coenzyme A (CoA)/ethylmalonyl-CoA/hydroxybutyryl-CoA (CETCH) cycle for the CO 2 fixation in _vitro._[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib13) He went further by encapsulating the CETCH cycle in cell-sized droplets using microfluidics as a chloroplast mimic to create an artificial photosynthetic system, and this “synthetic chloroplast” have the potential to outcompete natural photosynthesis.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib14) Although this system is primarily driven by microfluidic platform _in vitro_, it has paved the way for combining bioartificial photosynthetic systems and agricultural production.

### 3.2. Introducing carbon concentrating mechanisms

In addition to directly improving the [enzyme activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/enzyme-activity "Learn more about enzyme activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), introducing carbon concentrating mechanisms (CCMs) to increase CO 2 concentration around the Rubisco enzyme is another efficient way to improve the carbon fixation efficiency. [Carboxysomes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/carboxysome "Learn more about Carboxysomes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") encapsulate Rubisco and [carbonic anhydrase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/carbonic-anhydrase "Learn more about carbonic anhydrase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using a selectively permeable shell, and they are central CO 2-fixing factory of CCMs in [cyanobacteria](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cyanobacteria "Learn more about cyanobacteria from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). However, carboxysomes have a complex assembly, and it is difficult to directly transplant the complete genome of carboxysomes into plants to achieve the desired effect.

Prof. Maureen Hanson first reported the transplastomic tobacco line with knocked out natural tobacco Rubisco gene and inserted Rubisco and an internal carboxysomal protein.[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib15) This simplified carboxysome could form macromolecular complexes in the chloroplast stroma, and improved CO2 fixation rate. Prof. Cheryl Kerfeld successfully constructed a chimeric protein designed based on the carboxysome core in the [cyanobacterium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/cyanobacteria "Learn more about cyanobacterium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[Synechococcus elongatus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/synechococcus-elongatus "Learn more about Synechococcus elongatus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that structurally and functionally replaced four components required for the formation of carboxysomes. Carboxysomes constructed on this chimeric protein could also support photosynthesis.[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib16) These works provided some ideas for simplifying the construction of carboxysomes in plants. In addition to the study of carboxysomes, Prof. Stephen Long also showed that [transgenic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenics "Learn more about transgenic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[C3 plant](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/c3-plant "Learn more about C3 plant from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") soybean significantly increased photosynthetic CO 2 uptake and [dry mass](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dry-mass "Learn more about dry mass from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by transferring cyanobacterial [inorganic carbon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/inorganic-carbon "Learn more about inorganic carbon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") transporter B (ictB).[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2773237122000065#bib17) This also become another route for CCMs transplantation.

### 3.3. Nitrogen fixation in plants

Improving the biological [nitrogen fixation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nitrogen-assimilation "Learn more about nitrogen fixation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pathway and increasing the utilization rate of nitrogen sources by crops is also an important area for applying synthetic biology in [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agricu

enriched_points.point_title: Integrating synthetic biology with traditional practices
enriched_points.expanded_content: Combining synthetic biology with conventional farming enhances crop resilience, optimizes nutrient uptake, and promotes eco-friendly agricultural methods, creating sustainable and productive farming systems.

Implementing synthetic biology innovations, such as engineered microorganisms and improved crop traits, ensures stable food supplies while addressing environmental challenges and maximizing resource efficiency.
